text: Approximately 20% of individuals may not achieve meaningful increases in cardiorespiratory fitness in response to endurance training.
Genetics is the most potent biological contributor to training response variability, explaining approximately 50% of the variability in cardiorespiratory fitness changes.
Age, sex, and baseline cardiorespiratory fitness each explain a smaller proportion of training response variability compared to genetics.

Prescribing exercise intensity relative to a physiological threshold (e.g., ventilatory threshold) increases cardiorespiratory fitness response rates compared to prescribing intensity relative to maximum physiological values (e.g., maximum heart rate).

Methodological factors such as exercise type, volume, intensity, and intensity prescription method are modifiable and can alter response rates to endurance training regardless of an individual's biological predisposition.

Threshold-based exercise intensity prescription elicits more homogeneous acute physiological responses among individuals, which when repeated over time may produce more uniform chronic adaptations and reduce response variability.
